does: This paper proposes a method for removing specific visual knowledge from multimodal large language models while preserving other visual and textual knowledge. The approach freezes the language model backbone and fine-tunes only the visual module using two main components: a Contrastive Visual Forgetting mechanism that separates target visual concepts from retained ones in feature space, and a null space constraint that restricts unlearning operations to directions orthogonal to retained knowledge representations. The method addresses the core challenge of achieving precise knowledge removal in MLLMs where visual and textual modalities are tightly coupled.
connects: This work builds on the broader machine unlearning literature, which has focused on removing specific information from trained models while maintaining performance on other tasks. It extends prior unlearning methods, which traditionally operate on single-modality models, to the more complex setting of multimodal systems where knowledge spans vision and language. The paper responds to recent challenges in MLLM safety and alignment by adapting contrastive learning approaches and null space projection techniques from prior work, combining these methods to handle the interdependency between visual and textual knowledge that single-modality unlearning does not address.
enables: This work opens directions for safer multimodal AI systems by enabling selective removal of problematic visual content while maintaining general model capabilities. It points toward dynamic unlearning scenarios where models can iteratively forget multiple target concepts, and suggests extensions to other multimodal architectures beyond current MLLMs. The null space constraint approach could inspire new techniques for other model editing and knowledge removal tasks. The method also enables practical applications in privacy-preserving MLLMs, content filtering, and addressing bias or harmful associations in vision-language models without full retraining.
